ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for fried chicken gyoza with fries and mayo. Closest defensible parent anchor is 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03315595). The composite nature of this dish (gyoza + fries + mayo) means no single exact code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Dumplings, fried) is the closest defensible match for the gyoza component. The french fries and mayonnaise components are not separately coded; this is a composite dish approximation using the dominant component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fried gyoza dish with fries and mayonnaise.

serving.description: 1 plate (approx. 5–6 pieces gyoza + small fries + mayo)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, comprising 5–6 pieces of fried chicken gyoza, a small portion of french fries, and a side of Japanese-style mayonnaise, as typically served in a Singaporean Japanese casual dining or food-court context.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Potstickers/meat-filled dumplings fried (FDC #172741 area); French fries deep-fried (FDC #170720); Mayonnaise regular (FDC #172741) — component-weighted estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: fried chicken gyoza (approx. 55% by weight, referencing USDA SR Legacy entries for fried meat-filled dumplings/potstickers), deep-fried french fries (approx. 30% by weight, USDA FDC #170720), and regular mayonnaise (approx. 15% by weight, USDA FDC #172741). Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not reliably disaggregated for this composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 — analogous estimate based on wheat-flour dumplings and deep-fried potato entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken gyoza with fries and mayo. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) wheat-flour dumpling wrappers (GI ~65–70, analogous to white wheat pasta/dumplings per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008); (2) deep-fried potato/french fries (GI ~63–75 per University of Sydney GI Database); (3) mayonnaise contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. Weighted composite GI of approximately 65 is defensible. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (65 × 40 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 26.0 g (high GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium, representing ~39% of the WHO 2300 mg daily limit from a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to a half-portion or less, request reduced soy sauce and salt seasoning, and avoid adding extra mayonnaise, as cumulative sodium from soy sauce, seasoned fries, and mayonnaise can approach or exceed 1000 mg per full serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) with a high glycemic load per serving (~26 g GL per 250 g), driven by wheat-flour gyoza wrappers and deep-fried potato; 1.6 g dietary fibre per 100 g provides limited glucose-buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict this dish to a half-portion (approximately 125 g), pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side to increase fibre intake, and avoid repeat servings, as the high glycemic load per full serving is likely to cause a significant postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 18 g per 100 g with saturated fat 3.2 g and trans fat 0.2 g per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers 45 g total fat, 8 g saturated fat, and 0.5 g trans fat, alongside 900 mg sodium and 70 mg cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence only; the combination of deep-frying-derived trans fat, elevated saturated fat, high sodium, and high caloric density (260 kcal/100 g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and atherogenic risk, and regular consumption is not recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying of both gyoza and fries generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its deep-fried form; if gyoza is desired, request steamed or pan-fried preparation to reduce AGE formation and retain the anti-inflammatory benefit of ginger and garlic in the filling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken Gyoza with Mayonnaise and French Fries is a Japanese-origin snack or side dish popularised in Singaporean casual dining and food-court settings. The dish consists of 5–6 pieces of deep-fried chicken gyoza (Japanese-style dumplings filled with seasoned chicken mince, cabbage, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, enclosed in thin wheat-flour wrappers), served alongside a small portion of deep-fried french fries and a side of Japanese-style mayonnaise for dipping. The gyoza are deep-fried to a golden, crispy exterior with a juicy, savoury interior; the fries are crisp and lightly salted; and the mayonnaise adds a rich, creamy, slightly tangy contrast. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 260 kcal, 10 g protein, 18 g fat (3.2 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 16 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. A standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 650 kcal, 900 mg sodium, and a glycemic load of ~26 (high). Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to high sodium accumulation per serving, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to a medium GI and high glycemic load, and high concern for cardiovascular disease due to the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, elevated sodium, and high caloric density from deep-frying. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in its current preparation due to AGE formation from deep-frying. The dish contains gluten (wheat wrappers), egg (mayonnaise), and soy allergens. It is not vegetarian, vegan, halal-certified, or gluten-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken gyoza with fries?
What is the glycemic index of gyoza?
Is chicken gyoza suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in fried gyoza with fries?
Is gyoza with mayo good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging of USDA FoodData Central entries for fried meat dumplings, deep-fried french fries, and regular mayonnaise. The input GrabFood-sourced values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this estimate and have been retained after cross-validation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid disaggregation for this composite fried gyoza dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; the composite preparation makes reliable MUFA/PUFA estimation impractical without laboratory analysis. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reliably available for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish is sold on GrabFood in Singapore but no halal certification is documented in the input record. 4. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Sushi', 'Tempura', 'Grilled Fish') are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the output. 5. The dish is a composite of Japanese gyoza and Western-style fries, reflecting the fusion snack culture common in Singaporean Japanese casual dining chains. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.2 per 100 g is retained from the input; this is plausible for deep-fried products using partially hydrogenated oils and is consistent with USDA data for similar fried products. 7. This is a Japanese cuisine dish; mandatory multilingual requirement per G6 is Japanese native-script name only. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not mandatory for Japanese cuisine and are correctly left null. 8. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' as the dish is a composite with no exact ontology code available.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is canonical English ('Fried Chicken Gyoza with Mayonnaise and French Fries'). Japanese local name is present in native script as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine. Romanized form also provided. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi are not mandatory for Japanese cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (260), protein_g (10), fat_g (18), carbohydrate_g (16) all present and numeric. nutrient_source documents USDA FoodData Central component-weighted estimation with specific FDC references. No core macro is zero. No forbidden sources used.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.6), sodium_mg (360), saturated_fat_g (3.2), sugar_g (1.2) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a deep-fried composite dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (65), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (26.0), and gi_source all present. GI source cites University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis fully documented. GL calculation: (65 × 40 g available carb per 250 g serving) / 100 = 26.0.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 ('dumpling food product') uses match_type 'closest_parent' — valid per scoring model. FoodEx2 A0FJP ('Dumplings, fried') uses match_type 'closest_parent' — valid per scoring model. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters). Covers what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate), Cardiovascular Disease (high). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required. Rheumatoid arthritis retained as additional_condition.
